summary: Cantrill reviews two books about beating casino odds: 'Bringing Down the House' about MIT card counters and 'The Eudaemonic Pie' about physicists building a device to predict roulette. He finds the latter more compelling because the technical problem is harder and the protagonists more intellectually serious. The post is a light book recommendation that reveals his preference for rigorous technical challenges over clever schemes.
key_points: Compares two books about beating casino games through analytical methods
Prefers 'The Eudaemonic Pie' for its harder technical problem (predicting roulette via physics)
Contrasts the life trajectories of the protagonists from each book
Criticizes 'Bringing Down the House' for the author's self-insertion into the narrative
money_quotes: The book was brain candy in the purest sense: it was exhilerating and fun – but it definitely ruined my dinner.

Bass's subjects are more interesting to me, if only because the problem they're solving is so much harder: a group of physicists and computer scientists develop a device to give them an advantage over roulette. (After all, it's just Newtonian physics, right?)

And while the 'Bringing Down the House' protagonists seem destined for a life of overpaid corporate consulting and/or 12 step programs, the leader of the 'Eudaemonic' tribe, Doyne Farmer, now writes papers for academic journals.
I didn't drop fifteen bones to read 'The Making of Bringing Down the House'…
key_insight: The harder the technical problem, the more intellectually satisfying and ultimately meaningful the pursuit—a preference that distinguishes serious technologists from clever opportunists.
